faq_1_question: What is Duty Drawback and how can it reduce my customs costs?
faq_1_answer: Duty Drawback is a U.S. Customs program (19 U.S.C. § 1313) that allows you to recover <strong>99% of duties, taxes, and fees</strong> paid on imported goods that are later exported or destroyed. For retail brands, this means reclaiming costs on <strong>unsold inventory exported to consignees </strong> or <strong>customer returns shipped back overseas</strong>. It directly improves your landed cost margins and cash flow.
faq_2_question: Can non-US brands claim Duty Drawback on returned goods exported from the US?
faq_2_answer: <strong>Yes</strong>, if you meet three conditions: (1) You were the <strong>importer of record</strong> when the goods entered the US, (2) the returned goods are <strong>identical</strong> to the imported ones (or qualify under substitution rules), and (3) they are <strong>exported or destroyed</strong> within <strong>5 years</strong> of import. Many non-US brands overlook this—returns can generate <strong>6-8% cost savings</strong> on original import duties.
faq_3_question: How does Duty Drawback apply to US brands exporting inventory to overseas consignees?
faq_3_answer: If you import goods into the US and later export them <strong>unused</strong> to consignees (e.g., distributors, subsidiaries), you can claim drawback. <strong>Direct Identification</strong> (matching serial numbers) is simplest, but <strong>Substitution Drawback</strong> (using identical replacement goods) is more practical for retail. <strong>Pro tip</strong>: Track inventory by HTS code to maximize eligibility.
faq_4_question: What documentation is critical for Duty Drawback claims?
faq_4_answer: <strong>Non-negotiable records</strong>: (1) <strong>CBP Form 7501</strong> (entry summary) proving duties paid, (2) <strong>Commercial invoices</strong> and <strong>packing lists</strong>, (3) <strong>Proof of export</strong> (e.g., AES filings, bills of lading, or <strong>CBP Form 7512</strong> for informal exports), and (4) <strong>Inventory tracking</strong> linking imports to exports. For returns, <strong>RMA documentation</strong> is also key. <strong>Digital records</strong> are acceptable but must be <strong>retrievable for 5+ years</strong>.<br><br><strong>The TDR Advantage:</strong> Our team will provide all necessary support to create a compliant repository of documentation. With your consent, they will also access your ACE profile and pull down necessary data.
faq_5_question: How long does it take to receive Duty Drawback refunds?
faq_5_answer: <strong>CBP processes claims in 3-12 months</strong> on average. <strong>Accelerated refunds</strong>can reduce this to <strong>4-8 weeks</strong> if you're a trusted filer.<br><br><strong>The TDR Advantage:</strong> Our team will request Accelerated Payment privilege on your behalf.<br><br><strong>Finance teams</strong>: Plan for a <strong>6-month working capital cycle</strong>—but refunds are <strong>interest-free</strong>, so the ROI is effectively the full duty amount recovered.
faq_6_question: What percentage of duties and fees can I recover?
faq_6_answer: <strong>99% of duties, taxes, and most fees</strong> (e.g., Harbor Maintenance Fee, Merchandise Processing Fee). <strong>Exceptions</strong>: Some trade remedy fees (e.g., antidumping/countervailing duties) may have <strong>lower recovery rates</strong> or restrictions. <strong>Example</strong>: On $100,000 of duties paid, you'd recover <strong>$99,000</strong>—a direct boost to your P&L.
faq_7_question: What are the top reasons Duty Drawback claims get rejected?
faq_7_answer: <strong>Avoid these mistakes</strong>: (1) <strong>Missing export proof</strong>(e.g., no AES filing for informal shipments), (2) <strong>Inadequate inventory tracking</strong> (can't link imports to exports), (3) <strong>Late filings</strong> (5-year deadline from import), (4) <strong>Incorrect HTS  classification</strong> mismatches, and (5) <strong>Using non-identical substitution goods</strong>.<br><br><strong>The TDR Advantage:</strong> Our team will support you implementing a <strong>drawback-specific ERP workflow</strong> before claiming.
faq_8_question: Should we file Duty Drawback claims in-house or use a customs broker?
faq_8_answer: <strong>DIY</strong>: Feasible if you have <strong>an ABI software and robust customs expertise</strong>. <br> Alternatively, <strong>TDR</strong> takes care of all the heavy lifting, the EDI connection with Customs and typically increases recovery rate by 30% through optimization.
faq_9_question: Are there audit risks with Duty Drawback, and how can we mitigate them?
faq_9_answer: <strong>Yes</strong>—CBP audits <strong>~5% of claims</strong> annually. <strong>Red flags</strong>: High claim volumes, frequent substitution use, or inconsistent documentation. <strong>Mitigation</strong>: (1) <strong>Conduct a pre-filing audit</strong> of your records, (2) <strong>Use ACE Drawback module</strong> (CBP's preferred system), (3) <strong>Document everything</strong> (e.g., why substitution was used), and (4) <strong>Designate a single POC</strong> for CBP inquiries. <strong>Penalties</strong> for negligence can include <strong>denied claims</strong> or <strong>fines up to the duty amount</strong>.<br><br><strong>The TDR Advantage:</strong> Trust our expert team to submit fully compliant claims to CBP</strong> and reduce the risk of Customs audits.
faq_10_question: Why should I choose TDR as my drawback partner?
faq_10_answer: <p><strong>Dedicated retail duty drawback expertise:&nbsp;</strong>TDR is the only partner focused on crossborder retail drawback, with a specialist process built for eCommerce returns and re-exported inventory.</p><p><strong>Cost-effective for any retailer:&nbsp;</strong>No budget threshold and success-based fees mean TDR supports small and large drawback budgets, so retailers pay only when recovery succeeds.</p><p><strong>Simplifies global duty recovery:&nbsp;</strong>One partner handles US, EU, and UK duty mitigation without the customer needing a separate coordinator, reducing complexity and improving cash flow.</p>
